headline: Man Steals Boat, Drives It Six Miles Down The Interstate Before Realizing His Mistake
excerpt: A local man stole a boat and drove it six miles down the interstate before anyone, including him, questioned the plan.
byline: Filed by a deputy who clocked out forty minutes late because of this
body: At 2:14 this morning, Vice PD received seventeen separate calls reporting a twenty-two-foot center-console fishing boat traveling at a steady fifty-one miles per hour in the center lane of the interstate, running lights on, trolling motor down, no trailer, no visible means of propulsion beyond what investigators describe as "pure conviction."

The driver, later identified only as "a guy," told responding officers he had taken the boat from a marina because it was, in his words, "just sitting there, disrespectfully." When asked how he expected the boat to move on dry land, he reportedly paused for a long time and said, "I didn't think that far ahead, no."

Witnesses say the boat maintained highway speed for approximately six miles, navigated two exits without signaling, and came to a stop only when the man ran out of what he called "boat gas," which does not exist, and what investigators believe was simply momentum.

He was found sitting in the captain's chair, hazards on, waiting for what he described as "someone official" to explain what happens next. Someone official arrived. It did not go well for him. It went great for everyone else, who now have this.
The boat has been returned to its owner. The owner has requested it never be spoken of again. Vice City will not honor that request.

headline: Golf Course Evacuated After Man In Alligator Costume Reportedly ‘Just Wanted To Play A Round’
excerpt: A man in a full alligator costume caused a nine-hole evacuation and simply wanted to golf.
byline: Filed by the pro shop attendant who saw the whole thing and still isn't right
body: Emerald Isle Golf & Country Club was evacuated in full Tuesday morning after a man in a head-to-toe, professional-grade alligator costume was spotted approaching the third hole with a full set of clubs and, according to witnesses, "perfect etiquette."

Golfers fled. Groundskeepers fled. A wedding party using the clubhouse for photos fled, still in formalwear, and honestly got some great candid shots out of it. The man in the costume did not flee. The man in the costume waited patiently at the tee box for the group ahead of him to clear the green.

When confronted by course security, the man reportedly removed his mask just far enough to say, "I have a 10:40 tee time," and put the mask back on. He did, in fact, have a 10:40 tee time. It was under his real name. He had paid in full. Nothing in club policy prohibits alligator costumes on the course, a fact the club's lawyers have since confirmed and deeply regret confirming.

He was escorted off the property before completing his round. Course management has since added a section to the dress code. It is four pages long. It did not need to be four pages long. It is four pages long because of him.
He has not been identified. He has, however, already requested his tee time back for next Tuesday.

headline: Man Rescued From Billboard After Climbing Up To ‘Fix The Spelling,’ Which Was Not Actually Wrong
excerpt: A local man scaled a highway billboard at night to correct a typo that, on closer inspection, was never there.
byline: Filed by a fire crew that had a very long conversation about apostrophes
body: Fire crews responded just after midnight to reports of a man stranded roughly forty feet up the support ladder of a billboard advertising a local mattress outlet, having climbed up, in his words, "to fix the apostrophe situation."

The billboard reads: "YOUR NEW MATTRESS IS WAITING." There is no apostrophe on the billboard. There was never an apostrophe on the billboard. The man maintains, even now, even after the ladder truck arrived, even after the whole thing was over, that there absolutely should be one, and that this is "a hill he's willing to die on," a phrase he chose to say while forty feet in the air, which the responding captain called "not ideal messaging."

He was talked down over the course of about twenty-five minutes, during which he continued to explain, at volume, the proper use of the possessive apostrophe to a growing crowd of commuters who had stopped their cars specifically to watch this happen.
No charges have been filed. The mattress company has declined to comment on the grammar allegations but has confirmed the sign will not be changed.
The man was last seen accepting a blanket from paramedics and telling them, unprompted, that "it's ‘fewer,’ not ‘less,’ when you can count it," which nobody asked him.

headline: Downtown High-Rise Reports Break-In; Only Item Missing Is A Single Golden Toilet
excerpt: Thieves bypassed an entire penthouse's worth of valuables to remove exactly one solid gold toilet.
byline: Filed by a detective who has seen some things but not this
body: A break-in at a downtown penthouse left the unit almost entirely untouched — cash, art, and a safe containing what the owner describes only as "documents" were all left in place — except for one item: a fully functioning, solid gold toilet, professionally disconnected from the plumbing and removed without so much as a scuff on the tile.

The owner, who has requested to remain unnamed for reasons he described as "optics," says the toilet was worth "a significant amount of money, don't print the number," and that its removal must have taken "real professionals, plumbing professionals, this was not amateur hour."

Detectives note the thieves left behind two other bathrooms, both with regular, non-gold toilets, entirely undisturbed, suggesting this was not a general theft but a targeted operation with, in the words of one officer, "unbelievably specific priorities."

No suspects are in custody. Investigators are, however, asking anyone with information about the sudden appearance of a residential-grade gold toilet anywhere in the tri-county area to come forward, no questions asked about why you'd recognize one on sight.
The owner has already ordered a replacement. It is, we are told, "a little bigger this time."

headline: Petting Zoo Llama Goes Missing Same Night As Nearby Bank Alarm; Officials Insist ‘No Connection’
excerpt: A llama vanished the same night a nearby bank's silent alarm tripped twice, and officials would like everyone to stop asking about it.
byline: Filed by a reporter who has a corkboard now and isn't sorry
body: Sunny Acres Petting Zoo reported one of its resident llamas, a six-year-old named Kevin, missing from his enclosure sometime between 11 p.m. and 6 a.m. Monday. At 11:42 p.m. that same night, the silent alarm at Sunbelt Savings & Loan, four blocks away, was triggered twice in quick succession before being reset from inside using a valid employee code.

Police have stated, clearly and on the record, that there is "no connection whatsoever" between these two events, a statement they have now made four separate times, unprompted, to four separate reporters who did not ask.

Zoo staff describe Kevin as "food-motivated, occasionally stubborn, not what I would call a mastermind, but also not not that." Bank staff, when asked whether any employee badge access logs showed unusual activity that night, declined to comment and then, thirty seconds later, added that "nothing was taken, everything's fine, please stop calling."
Kevin was recovered two days later grazing peacefully in a public park a quarter mile from the bank, wearing, according to the zookeeper who found him, "a bandana that isn't ours."
The bank's vault was, for the record, untouched. Everyone involved would like this story to end here. It is not going to.

headline: Man Arrested For Attempting To Sell A Fighter Jet On A Local Classifieds Site
excerpt: A decommissioned fighter jet was listed for sale online for two thousand dollars, negotiable, local pickup only.
byline: Filed by an officer who screenshotted the listing before it got taken down
body: A listing titled "JET (fighter) — runs, some rust, no trades" appeared on a local classifieds site Sunday, priced at two thousand dollars, negotiable, cash only, local pickup only, described in full as: "Cool jet. Was going to fix it up but life got busy. Comes with the little ejector seat thing. Buyer must be able to move it."

The listing included four photos, one of which clearly showed the aircraft still bearing partial military markings, taken, investigators believe, on a private airstrip the seller does not own and has no documented connection to.

Officers arrived to find the man mid-negotiation with an interested buyer who had, remarkably, already brought a flatbed truck. When asked where the jet came from, the seller reportedly said, "That's kind of a long story, and also I'd rather not," which detectives noted was "not the reassurance we were hoping for."

The jet has been returned to its rightful, extremely relieved owner, who was previously unaware it had left the premises. The would-be buyer has asked whether anything else is available. He has been advised no.
The seller faces multiple charges. The listing, mercifully, has been removed. Several screenshots have not.

headline: Golf Course Alligator Named ‘Big Steve’ Now Has His Own Fan Club And A Reserved Parking Spot
excerpt: A twelve-foot alligator who took up permanent residence on the seventh hole now has merchandise.
byline: Filed by someone who bought the shirt and does not regret it
body: Big Steve, the roughly twelve-foot alligator who has lived in the water hazard on the seventh hole at Emerald Isle Golf & Country Club for the better part of three years, now has an official fan club, a laminated members card, and — as of this week — his own reserved parking spot near the clubhouse, painted and everything.

Club management, who initially attempted to have Big Steve relocated in his first year, gave up after what the head groundskeeper describes as "four failed removal attempts and one incident I'm not going to get into," and instead leaned all the way in.

Big Steve now appears on the club's letterhead. Big Steve has a bobblehead in the pro shop. Big Steve does not, notably, do anything, ever, except lie in the water and occasionally drift six inches to the left, and members insist this is the entire appeal.
"He's very calming," said one member, teeing off approximately eleven feet from twelve feet of alligator. "I've never once seen him move with intent. That's more than I can say for my ex-wife."
The fan club currently has over four hundred members. Big Steve remains, as far as anyone can tell, unaware of any of this, which the club considers his best quality.

headline: Beachgoers Flee After ‘Massive Creature’ Surfaces, Turns Out To Be A Very Large Manatee Named Gerald
excerpt: A beach panic over a sea monster resolved into a well-known, extremely relaxed manatee.
byline: Filed by a lifeguard who has explained Gerald to tourists more times than he can count
body: A crowded stretch of public beach cleared out in under ninety seconds Saturday afternoon after a large, dark shape surfaced roughly thirty feet from shore, prompting at least two people to scream the word "kraken" at full volume and one man to abandon a full plate of nachos, which local seagulls have since thanked him for.

The creature in question was Gerald, a well-documented, roughly eleven-hundred-pound manatee who has frequented this specific stretch of coastline for six years, is known personally to every lifeguard on staff, and has never once, in six years, done anything more threatening than slowly chew on seagrass with what witnesses consistently describe as "a face like he's already bored of this."

Lifeguards spent the following twenty minutes explaining, repeatedly, that Gerald is not a threat, is not a monster, is not, as one panicked tourist insisted, "clearly evolving," and is in fact beloved enough locally that there is a laminated photo of him behind the snack bar counter labeled simply "Gerald, be nice."
The nachos were not recovered. Gerald was last seen drifting further down the coastline at his standard cruising speed, which lifeguards describe as "slower than the tide, somehow."
No injuries were reported, aside from one gentleman's pride.
